paper_title: %\documentclass[pdflatex,sn-mathphys-num]{sn-jnl}% Math and Physical Sciences Numbered Reference Style

perturbations.why_wrong: In Kiselman's semigroup $K_n$, the non-commutative relation for $j < i$ is $a_i a_j a_i = a_j a_i a_j = a_i a_j$. Changing the result to $a_j a_i$ contradicts the standard definition of the idempotent relations where the product reduces to the generator with the larger index on the side of the absorption.

The text states that $\mathcal{L}$ coincides with the distance from the zero element. Moving toward the zero element by multiplying by a generator should decrease the distance, not increase it. Furthermore, the context mentions the hitting time at which a sequence attains its eventual value, implying a decreasing sequence of distances.

The context explicitly defines the ordering of indices as $i_1 > i_2 > \dots > i_k$. Reversing the order of multiplication in a non-commutative semigroup (implied by the study of specific presentation relations) changes the element $e_X$ and contradicts the established indexing convention.

Proposition 1.1 states that if $x a_k = f$ for $k \in \{2, \dots, n\}$, then $x = f$. If $R$ were defined using $a_n$ (where $n \geq 2$), then $R$ would only contain the element $f$, making its cardinality $|R| = 1$. This contradicts Proposition 1.2, which states $|R| = 1 + |K_{n-1}|$, implying $R$ must contain more than just the zero element.

The deletion endomorphism associated with a set $X$ is defined to remove elements in $X$, which corresponds to the identity on the complement $X^c$. The mapping to the Boolean matrix must use $I_{X^c}$ to be consistent with the definition of 'deletion' of $X$.

The lemma aims to show that applying the deletion operator for the set [m] is equivalent to applying it for [m-1] when followed by multiplication by a_m. Changing the right side to [m-1] makes the statement a trivial identity (A=A) and contradicts the subsequent proof steps where the reduction to the [m] index is the primary goal.

The original statement claims the equality holds for all j in {0, ..., i}. By changing the index in the operator to a fixed i, the universal quantification over j in the following line ('for all j...') becomes disconnected from the equation, and the inductive proof structure (which relies on j) would fail to support this specific fixed-index statement.

In the context of the deletion operator and Green's relations in this semigroup, the idempotent e_Y is defined based on subsets. The relation requires removing the indices already accounted for; using a union (\cup) instead of a set difference (\setminus) is inconsistent with the decomposition of the idempotent e_{[i]} into its constituent generators and lower-indexed idempotents.

The perturbation changes the decrement to an increment. This is inconsistent with the subsequent proof text which states that if the condition is met, the value should be lower (implied by the context of reaching a base state or decreasing indices) and contradicts the transition matrix in S0049 where probabilities p_i correspond to moving from state i to i-1.

The definition of B_x is perturbed to use index i-1. This is inconsistent with the definition of the map m(x) in S0041, which defines the minimum i such that x e_{[i]} = f, and the subsequent Theorem S0047 which claims the sets A_x and B_x are equal based on the same indexing.

perturbations.original:
\begin{equation}\label{transition:matrix}
    \mathcal{P} =
    \begin{pmatrix}
    1      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & \cdots & 0       & 0      \\
    p_1    & q_1    & 0      & \cdots & \cdots & 0       & 0      \\
    0      & p_2    & q_2    & \cdots & \cdots & 0       & 0      \\
    \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots  & \vdots \\
    \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots  & \vdots \\
    0      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & p_{n-1} & q_{n-1} & 0      \\
    0      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & 0        & p_n     & q_n
    \end{pmatrix},
    \end{equation}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{equation}\label{transition:matrix}
    \mathcal{P} =
    \begin{pmatrix}
    1      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & \cdots & 0       & 0      \\
    p_1    & q_1    & 0      & \cdots & \cdots & 0       & 0      \\
    0      & p_2    & q_2    & \cdots & \cdots & 0       & 0      \\
    \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots  & \vdots \\
    \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots  & \vdots \\
    0      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & p_{n-1} & q_{n-1} & 0      \\
    0      & 0      & 0      & \cdots & p_n     & 0       & q_n
    \end{pmatrix},
    \end{equation}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation introduces an extra column/entry in the final row. In a stochastic transition matrix, the sum of the entries in each row must be 1. By shifting q_n and leaving a 0, the structure of the tridiagonal matrix is broken, and it no longer matches the recursive definition L_j = g(L_{j-1}, I_j) where q_i is the probability of staying in the same state i.

The context states that the process remains in state $i$ for $k-1$ steps and then jumps to state $i-1$. Therefore, the transition probability must be $\mathcal{P}_{i, i-1}$, not $\mathcal{P}_{i, i+1}$.
